data.title: December CPI Inflation: The Narrative Can Change In A Blink
data.description:
Nothing changed on the price front in December 2025, as both the headline and core inflation figures came in unchanged from the month before. Yet, the entire narrative surrounding these numbers has altered. Here's how.

When I checked on the inflation figures for November 2025, the drop, particularly in core inflation, was noteworthy. Coupled with the then recent weakness in the labor market, there was a good chance that the Fed could well sound dovish, if not cut rates outright in January 2026. It also looked like a good time to remain cautious.

Cut to now, and the picture looks very different after multiple data releases since, including the labor market report for December 2025, the GDP print for Q3 2025, and of course the latest inflation figures. Here, I discuss what the changing overall context means for monetary policy and stock market investments.

Headline CPI inflation stays steady...

First, a closer look at the inflation figures. At 2.7% YoY, headline inflation stands bang at the annual average. The number for Q4 2025 stands at 2.75%, assuming the unreleased figure for October due to the government shutdown came in at the average of the numbers for September and November, at 2.85%.

Going by historical trends, PCE inflation, which is the Fed's preferred inflation measure, will come in even lower. The reserve, however, projects a rise of 2.9% YoY for Q4 2025 for the PCE index, which is bigger than that for even the CPI. This indicates that PCE inflation remains firmly likely to undershoot the forecast, making for an argument for a rate cut later this month.

...as does core inflation

Similarly for core inflation. It's an even more heartening development that core inflation, which reflects more accurately on the longer-term inflation trajectory, also remained steady at 2.6% YoY.

Compared with the annual average of 2.9%, core inflation looks even better, indicating that prices are coming firmly under control. Also, with the Fed's target for Q4 2025 for core PCE inflation at 3%, it's now more than likely that this inflation figure, too, would be softer than forecast, as the core CPI inflation for Q4 2025 is at 2.7% YoY.

Exceptionally muted probability of a rate cut...

But the inflation release doesn't convince the markets that the reserve is ready to cut rates just yet. At the time of the previous inflation print, there was over a 25% probability of a rate cut. The figure had fallen dramatically to 4.4% following the recent employment report. It has dwindled even further to 2.8% following the CPI report.

This could have something to do with the headline inflation print coming slightly higher than the consensus expectation of 2.6% YoY. Also, the number might not indicate a rise in CPI inflation, but it doesn't indicate a continued falling off either. Seen in conjunction with the small drop in the unemployment rate in December 2025, it's not hard to see why markets just don't see monetary policy easing anytime soon.

...but could the Fed still be dovish?

It's entirely possible that the data itself for at least October and November 2025 could have inaccuracies due to the government shutdown, which would give the Fed another reason not to cut rates. Also, the Fed's own projections for economic growth in 2026 are strong.

At the same time, it's hard to overlook that PCE inflation is expected to come in softer than expected by the Fed in 2025. And this is despite the impact of a potential upward pressure from tariffs. Also, even with the latest drop in the unemployment report, labor market softening over the past year is evident. This suggests that the Fed can keep it tempered while with a bent towards the dovish side.

Markets influenced by earnings releases

The three key market indices were essentially indifferent, tending towards negative, at yesterday's close, with all of them seeing sub-1% drops. The Dow Jones Industrial Average Index (DJI) was most affected, with a 9.8% drop, while the S&P 500 (SP500, SPX, SPY) and NASDAQ 100 Index (NDX) (QQQ) saw trivial softening (see Table 1 below).

While the market response is entirely in line with the unchanged CPI inflation figures, it's worth bearing in mind that the direct correlation between the indices and macroeconomic data and events can be muddled during earnings season. Yesterday, both JP Morgan (JPM) and Delta Air Lines (DAL) released their quarterly results. Both disappointed investors, with a 4.2% drop in JPM and a 2.2% correction in DAL.

The likely influence of these reports is evident from the fact that financial services saw the biggest correction among all sectors, of 2%, in yesterday's trading (see Table 2 below). It doesn't help that the CPI print doesn't provide any impetus for a rate cut later this month either.

Look towards cyclicals

A rate cut might not be coming this month, but monetary policy still stays in the easing cycle. This can be stimulating for an economy, which is expected to see a healthy performance in 2026 anyway.

This is one reason why I turned bullish about the S&P 500 for this year recently. Drilling down to the sectors, it should be no surprise that cyclicals are expected to be the likely gainers. In fact, despite the sector's latest wobble, financial services, as captured in the State Street Financial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLF), shows the most potential. Its forward P/E ratio showed the biggest undervaluation compared to its 5-year average levels (see section on "More sectors are undervalued than not" in the link).

Another sector to consider is materials, as the State Street Materials Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLB), which might have underperformed the S&P 500 over the past year, but on its own it didn't do too badly with a 14.4% increase. With a positive outlook on the economy, commodity prices could well inch up this year. In any case, the ETF is tempered by big holdings in gold mining stocks like Newmont Corporation (NEM).

In conclusion

The key takeaway here is that while the latest inflation print has remained unchanged, the entire narrative around it now lends to a starkly different story, as I was saying right at the start. Key data points released since show more strength in the economy than not, which can keep the Fed held back from rate changes right now. It is, however, a time to look more closely at cyclicals, which could see a good year ahead.

data.title: Zoetis: Cheap Valuation And Attractive Product Launch Pipeline
data.description:
Investment action

I give a buy rating for Zoetis (ZTS) because of the upcoming product launches and a cheaper relative valuation vs. its closest peers. Once the near-term issues fade, which I expect them to eventually, the market should start to realize ZTS deserves a higher multiple.

Company description

ZTS develops, manufactures, and sells medicines and vaccines to veterinarians and livestock producers. It serves two main markets: (1) companion animals (dogs, cats, and horses) and (2) livestock (cattle, swine, poultry, and fish). ZTS has been making efforts to shift away from one-off products like vaccines toward chronic treatments for kidney disease, heart disease, and cancer.

Pipeline and CKD

This is the main anchor for ZTS’s bull case. Per management, ZTS has a pipeline of 12 potential blockbuster products over the next five years, with each blockbuster defined as >$100 million in annual sales. While there is no guarantee that all these products will work, I think it is encouraging that ZTS is targeting entirely new white space areas like oncology, cardiology, and renal disease, which has a combined total addressable market [TAM] of >$5 billion, and importantly, effective treatments barely exist today.

I think this strategy of targeting white spaces across several categories makes sense for three reasons. First, it structurally lowers risk. If all 12 pipeline assets were for the same condition, one failure could wipe out the entire growth story. By targeting different diseases, even if ZTS fails in one area, like cancer, it still has other opportunities. Second, because these are new markets, ZTS would not suffer from “cannibalism” by fighting for share with its own products in existing categories. Third, because there are not strong incumbent solutions, ZTS has a “low hurdle” to get over.

Of all the new markets, I believe the most important market in the near-term is renal disease, wherein ZTS has a potential breakthrough in chronic kidney disease [CKD]. In the Piper Sandler conference, management confirmed ZTS will be launching a first-in-class monoclonal antibody for canine renal disease in 2027, and I think this could be a game changer. For those that are unaware, CKD is one of the most common and serious conditions in pets, especially as they age, and yet treatment today is extremely basic (mainly just diet changes and some fluids). There is no effective drug that slows the disease progress or even cure it.

Just this market itself is worth ~$3 to $4 billion, so if ZTS’s drug can indeed solve this major challenge, it would have a very strong first-mover advantage that potentially allows it to monopolize this multi-billion-dollar market for years. This would be similar to Cytopoint in dermatology (a breakthrough injectable drug in veterinary dermatology made by ZTS), but potentially larger given how serious kidney disease is. Given there is a real risk of the pet dying when pets are diagnosed with kidney failure, owners are generally desperate for solutions and would likely pay any amount to save their precious one. To put it bluntly, this actually gives ZTS pricing power because there is really no low-cost alternatives.

Moreover, this CKD drug that ZTS propose is not a one-off fix. It is a recurring treatment (likely be given monthly for the rest of a pet’s life), and that gives ZTS a recurring stream of cash flow. The knock-on impact is that this would also drive up ZTS’s broader renal and diagnostics business, as vets would have a reason to test earlier and more often once a real treatment exists. Earlier testing leads to earlier diagnosis, which expands the number of pets eligible for treatment.

Competition in dermatology

A common bear case for ZTS is competition in the dermatology category, especially against its drugs like Cytopoint. I actually don’t think this is as much of as risk since ZTS already has a plan to launch a long-acting version of Cytopoint, which is expected to receive US approval in H2 2026 (per the Piper Sandler conference). Instead of injections every 4 to 8 weeks, this upgraded Cytopoint is designed to control itching for several months with a single dose. The implication here is that fewer injections mean fewer vet visits, and better convenience for both vets and pet owners.

You might ask why would vets prefer lesser visits, it is because the industry is currently facing a massive labor shortage, meaning to say capacity, and not demand, is the bottleneck. For short-term recurring routine injections that carry little margin, it means a timeslot wasted that can be used for a high-margin surgery. Essentially, the new Cytopoint solves this “yield problem” for vets. Also, for the vets, inventory management becomes a lot simpler since they don’t need to carry so much stock keeping units, which reduces storage needs and the risk of expired products (100% loss in this case).

Given that vets are already well aware of Cytopoint (pros, cons, and how it works), there shouldn’t be any major friction in getting then to adopt the new Cytopoint.

Valuation

I believe ZTS’s valuation is the biggest disconnect in the equity story. The stock trade at just 18.5x NTM PE vs. its historical average of 29x, and the way I see it, this discount is really driven by near-term non-structural issues. Specifically, the Librela deceleration and softer veterinary visit trends. Regarding Librela, the recent softness was driven primarily by "misperceptions" regarding safety that were amplified on social media. Essentially this is a panic that is not driven by a fundamental failure of the drug's efficacy. ZTS is already addressing this with educational campaigns, and we are already seeing signs of stabilization. As the social media narrative fades and the clinical data prove the drug’s efficacy, growth should recover.

As for the softness in veterinary visits, this is really a cyclical dynamic, not a structural one. High inflation and poor macro have caused consumers to tighten their belts, but if we take a step back and look at the long-term, the secular trend of "pet humanization" remains intact. More importantly, investors seem to be focusing on the wrong metric. With the shift toward long-term injectables (like the upcoming Cytopoint LA), the correlation between visit volume and revenue growth is going to get wider since a vet only needs to administer one injection that lasts multiple months. In other words, this metric is not going to be the best indicator for growth.

To give a better sense of things, consider that Elanco Animal Healthcare (ELAN) trades at ~23x NTM PE with the exact same revenue growth outlook as ZTS (mid-single-digits), but is half the revenue size, has lower adj. EBITDA margin (~10pts difference), and a much higher debt profile (~4x net-debt to EBITDA vs. ZTS at 1.3x). If anything, ZTS should trade at a premium when we compare side by side. I believe the catalyst for this re-rating is when the new drugs in the pipeline comes online. If that happens and multiple goes back to at least 23x, this is an easy 24% upside.

Risk

If pipeline launches are delayed, fail, or see slow adoption, growth could disappoint. Competition in dermatology is not something we can completely dismiss either. If there is a new groundbreaking drug, it could disrupt ZTS market position and hurt both growth and margin.

Conclusion

I am positive on ZTS because of its strong pipeline, defensible position in the dermatology category, and also because it is cheap on a relative valuation basis. I believe the market is too focused on short-term issues and is underestimating the impact of the upcoming launches and the Cytopoint upgrade.

data.title: OXLC: Dividend Still Covered But NAV Decline Can Continue (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Overview

Credit funds have historically remained one of the places in the market where an investor can collect a high dividend yield. Since credit funds do not rely on the success of equities to generate their returns, they can be a great place to hedge against the uncertainty of equities during periods of volatility. However, the higher interest rate environment has continued to put pressure on the health of the debt markets. Oxford Lane Capital (OXLC) continues to see its share price erode since the time of my prior coverage. When I previously covered OXLC, I issued a buy rating due to the increased investment activity that had the potential to drive higher earnings. Now that we've crossed into the new year, I wanted to revisit OXLC to assess its value proposition at the new lows.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that OXLC's share price has deteriorated by 41.4%. The fund has continued to erode investor capital, despite traditional indices hovering near all-time highs. Even when including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, OXLC still has a total return at a loss of above 24%. Following the declines, OXLC now offers a massive starting dividend yield of 32.3% and issues payouts on a monthly basis. Unfortunately, I remain cautious about the overall sustainability of payouts in the near term.

Additionally, OXLC now trades near one of the deepest discounts to NAV valuations over the last ten years. While this can potentially be an indication that it's an attractive time to accumulate, I believe that the discount is actually a direct reflection of the challenges that OXLC continues to face. The underlying NAV continues to trend downward, and new investment activity was disappointing in the most recent quarter. Unfortunately, I believe that the success of OXLC will be heavily influenced by the direction of interest rates over the next twelve months. So, let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that OXLC implements to generate its earnings.

Financials & Performance

According to OXLC's latest portfolio overview, the fund has investments at a fair value of $2.59B that are spread across 298 different portfolio investments. OXLC is unique because it focuses almost exclusively on the equity tranche of CLOs (collateralized loan obligations). These are pools of different loans that have been bundled together and broken down by rating. Instead of lending money directly to companies, OXLC essentially buys the riskiest slice of the CLO structures managed by other management firms. The breakdown by CLO investment type is as follows:

  • CLO Equity: 99%
  • CLO Debt: 1%

OXLC is unique because it is essentially a 'fund of funds,' since it holds CLO investments that are managed by other firms. Despite this, OXLC still aims to achieve diversity across different industries to minimize any sort of concentration risk. As we can see below, OXLC is most heavily allocated to High-Tech investments at 11.1% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to Services: Businesses and Banking, Finance, Insurance & Real Estate securities, which each account for 10.1% of assets, respectively. The combination of these top three industries nearly makes up a third of total assets, so investors should anticipate that these industries will have the greatest impact on OXLC's daily price movement and total return potential.

To get better clarity into the fund's financial health, I wanted to review the most recent Q2 earnings report. Most notably, core net investment income amounted to $1.24 per share. While this was an improvement from the prior quarter's core net investment income of $1.19 per share, it was also a large decline from the prior year's core net investment income of $1.60 per share. The biggest driver was a decline in GAAP net investment income, which includes the actual interest income that OXLC receives from its positions. Similarly, OXLC has consistently experienced unrealized depreciation in its portfolio over the last twelve months. In the most recent quarter, unrealized depreciation totaled $0.71 per share. By segment, the total investment income was as follows:

  • CLO Equity: $124.6M
  • CLO Debt: $3.7M

One of the challenges continues to be the higher interest rate environment. Despite the Fed initiating three separate 25 basis point cuts to interest rates in 2025, the federal funds rate still remains near the higher end of its ten-year range. Essentially all of OXLC's portfolio is structured on a floating rate basis, so a higher interest rate directly translates to a higher cost of debt for its CLO positions. While this can technically lead to higher earnings since OXLC would be collecting a greater level of net investment income, it can also deteriorate the health of these positions.

A higher cost of debt puts pressure on securities since they have a greater debt burden. This can lead to an increased risk of defaults the longer interest rates remain elevated. As we can see below, that introduces a sensitivity to higher rates. OXLC has essentially failed to maintain any sort of positive growth momentum since 2021, which is when interest rates were at near-zero levels. The idea here is that a lower interest rate means that operating spreads are much wider, which is an easier environment for borrowers and funds to grow base assets.

So while the hope is that interest rates will continue to trend lower over the next twelve months, it's hard to know which direction interest rates will go. Even if the Fed continues to carefully initiate smaller 25 basis point cuts, I question whether or not these smaller rate cuts are enough to provide relief for borrowers.

Additionally, investors need to understand that OXLC's high dividend yield doesn't automatically translate to high total returns over time. For instance, OXLC's share price has declined by nearly 50% over the last five years. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return sits at 22.7% over the same time frame. Therefore, investors should anticipate that most of their returns will come from the fund's ability to continue paying out a high rate of distributions.

Valuation Risk

When I previously covered OXLC about a year ago, the fund traded at a small premium to NAV of 3.9%. Following the continued price erosion, OXLC now trades at a discount to NAV of 10.92%. For reference, OXLC has traded at an average premium to NAV of 6.54% over the last five years. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that OXLC now trades at one of the deepest discounts to NAV valuations over the last decade. While this may seem like an attractive time for entry, there is nothing that indicates that the price-to-NAV discount cannot grow larger over the next few quarters.

My main concern is around OXLC's ability to offset its portfolio losses with new investment activity. For instance, OXLC was able to allocate $145.2M towards new investments during the quarter, which is a significant decline from the prior year's new investment activity of $540M. As the rate of new investment activity slows, the potential for higher earnings slows. Additionally, these new investments were offset by sales and repayments totaling $173.5M during the quarter. This ultimately resulted in a negative net investment activity of $28.3M for the quarter. As a result, OXLC's NAV has continued to decline over the last year. For reference, here are the quarter-end NAV amounts:

  • Q2 2025: $23.80 per share
  • Q3 2025: $24.10 per share
  • Q4 2025: $21.60 per share
  • Q1 2026: $20.60 per share
  • Q2 2026: $19.19 per share

In a scenario where interest rates remain elevated, OXLC is at risk of being impacted by rising defaults. For instance, S&P Global estimates that the default rate of speculative-grade securities can rise as high as 5.1% by September of 2026 in its pessimistic outlook. Even if OXLC's investments aren't directly impacted by defaults, further deterioration of the debt markets can cause OXLC to decline.

The caveat here is that OXLC still has ~$366M of newly purchased CLOs that have not made their first payment yet. So, while the NAV and earnings have declined in the short term, we may eventually see these investments materialize into higher earnings over the next few quarters, according to the latest earnings call transcript.

As of September 30, we held approximately $366 million in newly issued or newly acquired CLO equity investments that had not yet made their initial distributions to Oxford Lane. - Jonathan Cohen, CEO

Tight Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.40 per share, the current dividend yield sits around a massive ~32.3%. This high monthly distribution has been declared through March of 2026. If we take this monthly distribution and multiply it by three months, we get an estimated quarterly payout of $1.20 per share. As previously mentioned, core net investment income amounted to $1.24 for the quarter, which does indicate that the distribution is still supported by earnings at this time. However, this leaves very little wiggle room in the case where OXLC is impacted by defaults or earnings declining further.

The sustainability of the distributions is dependent on how successful management's new investments are. If interest rates do decline over the course of 2026, I anticipate that OXLC's core net investment income will also decline since the portfolio is generating a lower level of interest income. So, it is crucial that the new investment activity can create streams of new cash flow to offset any losses over the next few quarters. Therefore, I plan to continue measuring OXLC's portfolio shifts over the next quarter.

Lastly, investors need to consider the fact that distributions received from OXLC may not offer tax efficiency. Since distributions are being supported by CLO equity, the income is classified as ordinary dividends, which have the highest tax burden for investors. Therefore, a position in OXLC is best utilized within a tax-advantaged account for investors that want to minimize their overall tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am downgrading my rating on OXLC to a hold at this time due to the potential for continued NAV erosion in the near term. Although there is still a significant amount of new investments that have not made their first payment, there's no established time frame for when these new investments may start to materialize into higher earnings. Since the new investment activity has significantly slowed year over year, I believe that growth over the next quarter may be limited. Core net investment income still supports the distributions being paid, but coverage is tight and leaves little room for error. Since interest rates remain elevated, OXLC can be negatively impacted by defaults and limited growth opportunities, which can cause the earnings and NAV to decline in the near term. So, despite the larger discount to NAV valuation, I do not believe this is the best time for entry.

data.title: Blood In The Streets? 2 Oversold Growth Stocks You Should Consider Adding To Your Portfolio
data.description:
Introduction

At the time of writing, the overall market has been a bit choppy to start the new year. Me, personally, I believe this has to do with uncertainty surrounding Venezuela and comments made by President Trump on defense stocks halting buybacks & dividends.

Going forward, I remain bullish on the market for 2026, but think we will see some volatility along the way.

For someone like me, this is an opportunity to add to my current holdings, or potentially add new ones.

With the fresh start of a new all-income portfolio, I've been focusing on adding high growth stocks to my main portfolio.

And the two I mention in this article are high growth ones that I think are currently oversold. Those who follow me may also be surprised to know that neither of the two pay dividends.

In this article, I discuss why investors looking for attractively-priced growth stocks should add these two to their portfolio.

#1 Netflix (NFLX)

At the time of writing, Netflix is currently trading below $90 a share. I wrote an article back in December, stating why I was bullish long-term on the company. Since, NFLX is down 10% as uncertainty looms around the acquisition of Warner Bros Discovery (WBD).

I'm optimistic and think that the deal will eventually go through. Competitor Paramount Skydance (PSKY) has made several offers to WBD to no avail. WBD rejected PSKY's most recent offer, remaining committed to NFLX.

If WBD backs out, they would have to pay NFLX a termination fee of $2.8 billion. Because of this, I don't think WBD is going to back out of the current contract. According to prediction markets, they seem to be in agreement with me that NFLX will successfully acquire WBD, giving them a 59% chance.

With the acquisition, this adds to NFLX's already large subscriber base over 300 million. WBD currently has over 100 million subscribers and this gives NFLX access to iconic names like DC, Harry Potter, & Game of Thrones.

Many are also likely worried about the deal adding roughly $80 billion in debt to NFLX's balance sheet. But I think the company is in a good position with only $14.46 billion in current debt, relatively small compared to their market cap of $408 billion.

NFLX has no short-term debt due and had robust liquidity of $9.2 billion in cash on hand. The acquisition is expected to close in the next 12 to 18 months and be accretive by year two. And by year three, the deal is expected to add $2 to $3 billion in cost savings.

NFLX doesn't pay a dividend, and with the pending acquisition, I don't think is in its near future despite strong cash flows. Instead, the company focuses on buying back shares. But I also expect these to halt for a few years because of the acquisition.

EPS grew 26% year-over-year, while revenue increased 15.3% from $28.7 billion to $33.1 billion. Free cash flow grew 37.2%, a result of cash from operations growing double-digits from $5.8 billion to $8 billion.

And going forward, NFLX is expected to see solid growth. If the deal does go through, I suspect this could be significantly higher in 3 to 5 years post-acquisition.

I view Netflix as a long-term play. So, investors should expect volatility in the near to medium-term. Additionally, there's a chance that the deal doesn't get approved by regulatory bodies. If so, the share price could decline further.

Currently, the stock offers close to 40% upside, making them attractive for long-term investors seeking growth.

#2 AutoZone (AZO)

AutoZone is another high growth stock that despite their well-known business, often flies under the radar amongst investors. I covered the stock back in May of last year, assigning them a buy rating. Since, the stock is also down 10% at the time of writing.

AZO hit a new 52-week high in September, but has since sold off significantly. As a result, the usually high growth stock has underperformed, up less than 1% in the past year.

Unlike Netflix, who has a pending merger, AutoZone's share price has been impacted by declining financials as the company missed on both their top & bottom lines. In their FYQ1 earnings back in December, AZO saw their bottom line and gross margins decline despite a higher store count.

At first glance, this seems troublesome for the company. Margins and EPS were down despite more stores.

However, both were impacted by a $98 million LIFO charge during the quarter. It's no secret that inflation and tariffs have impacted the company as well.

Excluding this, EPS would have grown 8.9% according to management. Earnings per share was down 4.6% from $32.52 to $31.04. Sales, however, still grew 8.2%, with more stores being a contributing factor.

Gross margins declined year-over-year from 53% to 51%. For comparison, peer O'Reilly Automotive (ORLY) saw a 27 basis points increase to 51.9%.

For the quarter, same-store sales were up 4.7%, higher than the previous year's 1.8%.

Commercial and International same-store sales were 14.5% and 3.7% respectively. International sales were down from last year's quarter that saw these increase 13.7%.

But AZO remains in a comfortable financial position as evidenced by their share repurchases and growth in free cash flow. During the quarter, management repurchased $431 million worth of shares, or 1.5%.

They had $1.7 billion remaining on the current program, which I expect management to take advantage of the suppressed share price in the coming quarters. Free cash flow grew 11.5% from $565 million to $630 million year-over-year.

And I expect this to continue seeing solid growth as AZO expects an additional 350 stores for the fiscal year, spending $1.6 billion, up from $1.3 billion last fiscal year.

Current store count sits at 7,710, up from 7,387 the prior year. Buybacks and accelerated store growth should lead to strong, double-digit upside for the foreseeable future.

In Q1, they opened 53 stores, up from 34 last year's quarter. Thirty nine of these were domestic openings. The LIFO charge is also expected to impact Q2 earnings. Management stated they anticipate a $4.6 billion impact to revenue, margins to decline 140 basis points, and $2.70 impact to their bottom line.

As a result, AZO could see heightened volatility. But as previously mentioned, AZO is one of the most fundamentally sound businesses.

Leverage was 2.5x as debt decreased from $9 billion to $8.62 billion, small compared to their market cap of approximately $55 billion. ORLY's leverage was slightly lower at 2.4x.

From the chart below, AutoZone's margins are expected to rebound. As a result, they will likely see some solid upside over the long-term.

Currently, the stock offers close to 30% upside to their price target of nearly $4,300.

Risks & Conclusion

For the near-term, both Netflix and AutoZone will likely remain volatile. NFLX due to the pending merger and AZO because of declining margins and profits.

Despite my optimism, NFLX's pending acquisition could eventually be disapproved. If so, I expect more volatility, potentially into the $70s.

Furthermore, because of their size, growth could also be hindered, leading to underperformance. Tariff uncertainty will also continue to impact AZO as the company continues to focus on international store openings.

However, both Netflix and AutoZone are high growth companies that I believe are poised to see strong upside over the next 2 to 4 years. As a result, I believe investors should consider them and rate both a buy.

data.title: Adobe: Another Step In The Wrong Direction
data.description:
As you can see in the rating history chart below, I have changed my mind on Adobe Inc. (ADBE) quite a few times back in 2025. However, a recurring theme in the second half of the year was that despite good adoption numbers, AI is getting monetized ineffectively. In September, I downgraded the stock to a sell and since then the stock is down around 9%. Today, we'll take a look at their most recent results to see if there are improvements in key areas.

In the below analysis, it is shown that monetization issues are persisting for the company. Innovation, adoption, and the partnership with OpenAI are encouraging for their AI efforts but with revenue growth slowing and guidance for FY2026 being soft, Adobe is failing to convert technological wins into financial wins. This explains why the valuation has continued to fall and in my view it would be unwise to call the stock a bargain right now. With no improvement in sight for their AI monetization, I have decided to reiterate the sell rating.

AI Innovation & Adoption Continues

Digital Media

Before going into the financials, let's briefly go over a sample of AI highlights from their two segments. Within their Digital Media business, Adobe is innovating in generative AI models as well as agentic experiences. They reported that their Firefly Image 5 model is "performing incredibly well with generation quality, native 4- megapixel resolution and industry-leading prompt-based editing capabilities." This is important as it indicates the company has mitigated AI-disruption rather well.

For creators and creative professionals, Adobe reported that they signed 100 new deals for Firefly and this shows that adoption is going well. One of Adobe's key measures for AI usage is "Generative Credit" and in Q4, they saw a 3x increase QoQ indicating that activity is strong. Lastly, it was reported that Adobe is partnering with OpenAI to bring flagship creativity tools to ChatGPT. This deal should increase usage and lead to better growth over time.

Digital Experience

In terms of their Digital Experience business there were also some AI highlights. For example, they released 6 new AI agents that are "powered by AEP Agent Orchestrator to transform how businesses build, deliver and optimize marketing campaigns and customer experiences." This shows that they are not static and are actively looking to expand their solutions portfolio.

Furthermore, the company stated that their Adobe Digital Index data showed that generative AI traffic was up 760% in the 2025 holiday season at the time their earnings came out and so once again activity is high. For more details regarding their AI developments, I encourage readers to take a look at their earnings call as this brief discussion is meant to show that the company is continuing to innovate and see good adoption. Now on to the tough part. Monetization.

Record Revenues But Growth Slows

For FY2025 Q4, Adobe generated revenues totalling $6.19 billion which is a new record for the company. While that is a welcome sign, note that the growth rate of 10% is a disappointing slowdown from the previous quarter. Therefore, questions remain regarding the company's AI monetization effectiveness. For Digital Media, growth slowed from 12% in Q3 to 11% while Digital Experience growth remained unchanged in reported terms. Overall, it is hard to argue that there isn't sluggishness. Expectations clearly weren't too high for them as they beat analyst estimates by $83.11 million.

As a clue to where revenues are headed, the company reported total RPOs worth $22.52 billion. This is a 12% increase in constant currency. As for cRPO, there was a 10% increase also in constant currency. While these figures are overall respectable, do note both of these growth rates are unchanged from Q3. Therefore, so far there aren't any signs in the top line results nor the RPO figures that the company has improved their monetization of AI. Business has remained stagnant and is not expected to accelerate significantly in the near future it seems.

Margin Is Stable

In terms of profitability, let's calculate the change in their non-GAAP operating margin. When dividing their non-GAAP operating income by revenue, we get 46% which is very strong. For the prior year this metric is calculated to be 46% as well and so there has been minimal change vis-a-vis corporate efficiency. Nonetheless, Adobe beat bottom line expectations by $0.10 as they reported an adjusted EPS of $5.50. While operating margin steadiness is probably a better reflection of profitability, it is of course a welcomed sign to see EPS growth accelerate. Let's move to cash flows and capital returns next.

Buybacks Are Quite Generous

In terms of cash generation, Adobe did a respectable job. Operating cash flows were reported to be $3.16 billion, up 8.2% YoY. This does trail both the top and bottom line growth rates and so there is definitely room for improvement. While the company doesn't pay a dividend, their capital returns are significant via stock repurchases. In Q4, as shown above, they bought back $2.474 billion of stock. With operating cash flows of just a bit over $3 billion, this is quite an aggressive capital return policy.

On one hand, shareholders obviously benefit directly from these buybacks. However, on the other hand, for a tech company that's aiming to be an AI leader in the digital creation space, perhaps investors may want to see a bit more reinvestment rather than just distribution. These are the two sides to the coin and it is something to think about.

Full Year Guidance Disappoints Me

Adobe provided both annual and quarterly guidance that was above analyst expectations. Those expectations likely weren't high, however. For a better long term view, let's focus on their full year projections. For total revenue, the range shown in the above table implies growth of 9.4% at the midpoint. That is a disappointment in my view as that is a slowdown from FY2025's already quite sluggish 11% increase.

In terms of ARR growth, their FY2026 expectation of 10.2% would also be a slowdown from FY2025's 11.5%. When looking at revenue and ARR guidance together, I would say there are no good indications that business will pick up. Monetization woes are set to continue for the foreseeable future. Lastly, for adjusted EPS, Adobe gave a midpoint of $23.40. That would be an 11.7% increase and would be yet another deceleration from FY2025's growth rate.

As a whole, I consider FY2026 guidance to be soft. In my view, FY2025 already wasn't a great year as AI adoption didn't really translate into financial performance. Now, looking forward to FY2026, not only will there not be improvement, the company may in fact be taking another step in the wrong direction.

Valuation Slide Justified

The forward P/E hasn't had any time to regroup at all. As you can see above, the multiple has seen quite consistent contraction in recent memory and while the stock seems cheap, I would be hesitant to call it a bargain.

As in my previous articles, Adobe is doing a fine job at innovating in AI and they are seeing some good adoption and activity statistics. The new partnership with OpenAI could provide another boost. However, there really is an issue with monetization. Despite their efforts in generative and agentic AI, results are not coming through as evidenced by sluggish top line growth. Margins remain stable and so it's not like the company's poorly run but until there is evidence that AI is improving the trajectory of the business, I would say the stock is unlikely to show outperformance.

Oftentimes, companies can focus on innovating and creating new services and the financials will take care of themselves. This is clearly not the case with AI. Adobe likely needs to rethink parts of their business model so that the strong adoption and good activity ends up being more meaningful to investors.

Final Thoughts

In my view, Adobe has quite successfully prevented AI from disrupting their business. Their push into generative and agentic AI has helped them remain relevant in the digital creation space. While AI is no longer considered a potential headwind for the company, it also isn't a particularly strong tailwind. There are no good indications that the company's AI push is paying off other than maintaining their sluggish growth rate and so I would say the valuation contraction shown above is really quite logical. Until an inflection point becomes apparent, I believe stock underperformance may continue and that's why I'm reiterating my sell rating.

data.title: AMD: The Trillion-Dollar Rack-Scale Arbitrage Behind The OpenAI Deal
data.description:
I have a strong buy for Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) stock based on its shift from a chip component challenger to a structural tech of the AI economy. The reason behind this rating is the merging of the Helios rack-scale architecture, the strategic 6-gigawatt deal with OpenAI, and the vertical integration of ZT Systems. This triad creates a stable bridge to $20+ non-GAAP EPS and a $1 trillion addressable market by 2030. Even to match 2030 EPS growth, AMD's stock price has to jump ~362% in the next 5 years (2026-2030) relative to the state at the onset of the AI Wave. This outlook is built on a >80% CAGR in the AI and >60% CAGR in the Data Center segment revenue and gross margin expansion toward 58%. For the thesis, major risks are delays in the MI400 series rollout, HBM supply cap, and geopolitical issues impacting China-side revenue recovery.

AMD Stock Forward Technicals Indicate a +120% Price Upside in 2026

AMD’s stock price trend (weekly frame) marks a constructive consolidation within a bullish channel (diagonal grey lines). AMD stock is currently taking support on an ascending trendline support zone going towards resistance of $260 (by 2026). The current horizontal support point of $193 is in line with the 61.8% Fibonacci retracement of the prior bullish impulse leg. The fan lines initiated from April 2025 lows project aggressive upside targets with immediate resistance at (recent swing high) $260. A stable weekly breakout above the $260 level can technically validate a run toward the 1.618 Fibonacci extension at $365 (by Q3-2026) and the 2.118 Fibonacci extension at $450 (by 2026’s end). The 6-week EMA is currently crossing down the 13-week EMA. This seems like a precondition for an extended consolidation between $260 and $193 before an uptrend resumption.

More so, the Stochastic Oscillator [SO] (black line) is hovering near the 20 level and approaching oversold indications. These marks suggest that the ongoing short-term bearish momentum is nearly exhausted. Hence, a bullish crossover of the %_K line (black) above the %_D line (red) from the ~20 zone can indicate forward price appreciation for AMD stock. To me, it is indicating that the upcoming weeks can provide an optimal risk-reward entry point for long-positional momentum.

Along with that, the MACD histogram is pointing out a reducing negative momentum even though the MACD line (black) is currently moving below the signal line (red). The flattening of the histogram bars points to a deceleration in selling pressure. On a forward basis, a bullish convergence and subsequent crossover above the zero line will serve as a high-confidence confirmation signal (to me) for the next leg of the long-term uptrend.

To support the consolidation argument, the Relative Strength Index [RSI] is reading ~51.25. RSI is firmly in the neutral zone. This state is indicating a continued consolidation pattern as AMD stock has corrected price excesses without entering a breakdown (as of now). I want to highlight that RSI has high headroom to run before overbought levels 70+, which is supporting the thesis that AMD stock has enough energy for a move toward the $260 range.

The Rack-Scale TCO Arbitrage Strategy

The strongest catalyst that will drive AMD’s stock price upward over the long term is the Rack-Scale Total Cost of Ownership [TCO] Arbitrage. This catalyst is supported by ZT Systems’ acquisition and the deployment of the Helios (platform). Wall Street focuses on chip-to-chip comparisons (MI350 vs. Blackwell), in my opinion. However, the true economic battle is being fought at the cluster level. AMD is selling cheaper GPUs, and it is engineering an open-standard infrastructure that breaks the proprietary tax of closed networking ecosystems [like Nvidia (NVDA)]. In my opinion, AMD’s current market valuations do not price in the shift from selling silicon to selling AI factories. By integrating ZT Systems' design expertise, AMD has insourced the ability to build hyper-customized liquid-cooled rack-scale products that integrate the MI450 accelerator, Venice CPUs, and Pensando networking into a unified compute unit.

This configuration creates a TCO arbitrage (80% TCO savings) for hyperscalers/enterprises beyond a forced unified high-margin proprietary interconnect tax (NVLink). AMD’s focus on the Ultra Accelerator Link (UALink) and Ultra Ethernet Consortium standards allows hyperscalers like OpenAI (OPENAI), Microsoft (MSFT), and Meta (META) to mix and match standard networking gear and is ticking near-monolithic performance. This reduces the cost per token for inference and training, possibly by 20-30% at the rack level (against Vera Rubin). The cross confirmation of this thesis lies in the 6-gigawatt deal with OpenAI (announced at the Financial Analyst Day). This is an architectural deal. Six gigawatts of compute is a massive infrastructure buildout that requires deep hardware-software co-design. OpenAI's deployment of MI450 at this scale indicates that AMD’s open ecosystem has crossed the threshold of good enough to be architecturally superior for cost-sensitive, massive-scale inference and training workloads.

Moreso, this strategy has a stickiness moat. Once a hyperscaler optimizes its data center thermodynamics, power delivery, and software stack (ROCm 7.0) for the Helios rack architecture, the switching costs increase. So, this shifts AMD’s revenue quality from cyclical component sales to recurring long-term infrastructure upgrades. As the AI market bifurcates into bleeding-edge training (competitor stronghold) and massive-scale inference/fine-tuning (open standard zone), AMD’s TCO arbitrage can capture the latter, which, in my opinion, can be the larger volume market by 2027. Wall Street is currently pricing in AMD as a second-source chip vendor. Now this strategy makes AMD a primary infrastructure architect that is warranting a large valuation multiple expansion.

AMD’s AI Supercycle Adjusted PEG-Based Valuation Indicates ~$350 By 2026

I apply a PEG-based valuation adjusted for the AI Supercycle. AMD has guided for >35% total revenue CAGR and >$20 non-GAAP EPS by the 2027-2028 timeframe. I consider the high-end consensus 2027 EPS of $13.4 (scaling up from the $3.87-$4.18 consensus for 2025 and $5.32-$8.02 for 2026) and apply a forward P/E multiple of 31.15x (average EPS during the AI Wave) justified by the possible high growth for revenue and margin expansion to 58%. This gives the fair value of $13.4 EPS x 31.15 x P/E, $417.4/share by 2027. Discounting it back to 2026 at a 19.3% discount rate yields a 12-month target of ~$350.

AMD stock is trading at ~$203.17 (as of now), and it means a forward P/E of ~51.2x on 2025 earnings. However, the forward non-GAAP PEG ratio, at a revenue CAGR of 80%+ in the AI and 60%+ in the Data Center segment, can drop below 1 on a segment-weighted basis as the current expected total revenue growth rate for 2026 is 31.37% below AMD’s long-term model. It indicates AMD stock is undervalued relative to its forward AI growth.

The Agentic AI Efficiency Paradox

The long-term threat to AMD's stock price is the Agentic AI Efficiency Paradox that can inadvertently cannibalize the TAM for high-end accelerators. The thesis builds AMD’s bullish case on a huge demand for compute ($1 trillion TAM). I am not ignoring the logic that there is a technological risk that rapid software optimization and the shift toward Small Language Models [SLMs] running on CPUs can decelerate the need for massive GPU clusters faster. Management itself pointed out that Agentic AI drives higher demand for CPUs [EPYC] because agents require general-purpose compute to interact with databases and enterprise tools. Although this boosts AMD’s server CPU market share (currently 40% and targeting >50%), server CPUs contain lower margins and average selling prices [ASPs] compared to Instinct GPUs.

In my opinion, if the target industry comes to the conclusion that the majority of enterprise AI workloads can be handled by highly optimized+quantized SLMs running on CPUs/specialized NPUs (like Ryzen/Radeon AI) rather than heavy power-consuming GPUs. Then the projected explosion in Data Center GPU demand may not happen. If inference workloads (on which AMD is banking for its MI400 ramp) become so efficient that they no longer require rack-scale GPU setups, the utilization rates of existing clusters will increase. This can delay new CapEx cycles. What I consider a possibility is that Enterprises/Hyperscalers will seek to cut costs and might lean heavily into this efficiency.

Apart from all these, memory dependency poses a correlated risk, as there is a critical role for HBM3E and forward HBM4 in the MI350/MI400 roadmap. Severe shortages in DRAM and NAND shift pricing power to memory vendors [Micron (MU), SK Hynix]. If HBM costs skyrocket due to supply caps, AMD’s bill of materials [BOM] will inflate. Unlike Nvidia’s pricing power to pass these costs onto clients, AMD’s gross margins have to absorb them to maintain market share (as AMD’s value proposition is TCO savings). If rising component costs erode AMD’s gross margin advantage with a potential shift toward lower-margin CPU inference, these can severely compress the valuation multiple and potentially drive AMD's stock price down.

AMD Stock Pre-Q4 and Post-Q4 Alpha Protocol

Pre-Q4 Earnings Strategy:

The time leading up to AMD’s Q4 earnings can be a window to accumulation on price weakness. AMD stock has pulled back and is testing the $193 support zone. Bulls should look for the MI308 China Export updates for clarification on licensing (as a risk factor) catalyst. The pre-earnings focus should be to establish a core position, as the market will be pricing in risks regarding the ZT Systems integration. Any pre-announcements confirming seamless integration can be viewed as a bullish sign.

Post-Q4 Earnings Strategy:

The post-earnings reaction will likely be dependent on two main metrics. One is Data Center GPU revenue outlook and gross margin. In my opinion, if AMD guides Data Center revenue above the current quarterly run rate and confirms gross margins expanding (~54% or more), this validates my TCO Arbitrage thesis. Bulls can aggressively add to long positions at/post-earnings. If revenue beats but margins contract due to HBM costs/aggressive pricing to win share from Nvidia, then it can lead to AMD stock to drop but the long-term thesis (2026/2027 ramp) remains intact if the OpenAI deal milestones remain on track. At the earnings, bulls should monitor for specific updates on the MI355 volume ramp. Any delay in the H2-2025 volume ramp is a red flag to my thesis.

_

To conclude, AMD stock is a strong buy in my stance. AMD has successfully diversified its revenue and secured deals [OpenAI, Meta, Oracle (ORCL)] to guarantee volume for its upcoming product cycles. The technicals suggest a solid floor at $193, and fundamentals point to a massive earnings expansion over the long term. For my thesis, mid-to-long-term forward monitorable elements are Gross Margin Progression to validate pricing power, the MI450 Timeline (any slippage from the H2-2026 launch is a thesis breaker), and HBM supply (I will monitor Micron/SK Hynix earnings/news for signs of big memory supply bottlenecks that can cap AMD's production).

data.title: PDI's Income Engine Remains Intact, Even As Total Returns Normalize
data.description:
The PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (PDI) is a Buy as the macro remains broadly supportive for its income engine. Investors need to align total return expectations to just about double digits, because that is where long-term rolling returns converge generally - particularly investors who are looking at PDI from a long-term lens.

Those return levels are still strong and overshadow the lack of any material NAV-compounding outlook at the moment. If the outlook is income and income durability (with reduced drawdown risks), PDI sits at the top end of the income spectrum today. The only thing to keep in mind is that total returns may lag what we have seen in the past 2-3 years - a moderation that is already starting to play out.

What the Fund Does

The fund's primary objective is current income, and this should be the core of how to gauge PDI's thesis at any point in time. But while capital appreciation is not a core objective, we have to keep an eye on price and NAV drawdown possibilities for risk management.

PDI employs a dynamic and unconstrained multi-sector fixed income strategy across non-agency mortgages (policy minimum 25%), high-yield credit, EM sovereign/corporate and IG credit, agencies, and derivatives. The core income generation therefore relies on credit and mortgage carry and amplification using moderate leverage. Active calls on duration management and cross-sector allocations become important. But the most important lever driving returns is the macro situation - specifically short-term funding costs, interest rate volatility, and the direction of mortgage and credit spreads.

Structurally, PDI distributes income in the majority of cases. At the same time, it does not rely on ROC as a return tool - although accounting ROC can be seen from time to time (caused by, say, mark-to-market timings, not systematically NAV eroding). The emphasis on distributing income and not destroying NAV by payouts at the same time implies NAV floors unless macro shocks (spreads and rates or liquidity) emerge. Similarly, the only route to NAV recovery or growth is dependent on regimes where mark-to-market gains exceed its very high distributions, after absorbing very high expenses. This NAV expansion, therefore, is a very rare phenomenon and often just a recovery after shock (like, say, a rebound in distressed assets bought cheap) or when spreads tighten after stress. Or when funding costs fall faster than asset yields.

As per Q3 2025 data, leverage levels are now moderate at ~28.8%. The leverage appears lower today compared to a year back when it was ~35%, or in January 2024 when it was even higher at ~40%. This in itself sets expectations to ground total return expectations down from what the mechanical amplification could allow in the past couple of years. On the other hand, the drawdown risks are also shallower.

The effective duration now is ~3.4 years, tilting towards a short duration - making it moderately sensitive to interest rate changes. This also shifts key risks and opportunities towards rate volatility and speed - like a sharp hike cycle in 2022 or a knee-jerk cut post-COVID. We are clearly not in either of those regimes today, so income sustenance looks more reliable.

The third data point that is important to note is a very high ~4.5% expense structure (because of leverage, hedging, and turnover) that drags total returns even if it does not directly hurt income.

Historical Data and Implications Today

The premium to NAV charts show that premiums are default for PDI given its high quality and magnitude of income potential. A ~15% yield retained for 7 years de-risks the capital deployed entirely. Calm income regimes have therefore meant persistent premiums for years. Even in shock regimes like that in 2022-23, the gap shrinks, but significant discounts are not seen consistently in the past decade. Today's premium levels are not higher than average. So premium pressures are not material for fresh entries now.

The yield charts also show stability overall. Spikes coincide with price collapses - mechanical uplift, not absolute payout growth. Yields also expectedly shrink following normalization periods. Today's yields are high even after significant rerating in the past couple of years (not because prices have collapsed), signaling a strong income engine. I expect the yields to stay intact as the funding cost volatility phase seen in 2022-23 is behind us. Policy rates today are higher for longer but stable - something that helps PDI's structure as discussed before (rate volatility over levels). There is no stress visible in the repo and financing markets either.

Total return charts are highly volatile, and outcomes are highly dependent on entry timing. The average 5-year total returns have generally trended towards low double digits to just about double digits. I expect the forward expectations to be in line with that average. Yields of ~15% should be sustained. Premiums should not be a drag. The NAV could drift lower due to expenses and mark-to-market corrections after payouts and should witness recoveries in phases when spreads tighten or funding costs ease further.

The moderation from the total returns of ~33% in the past 3 years is rooted in three core factors that are altered today, not stress. The premium upgrade regime is no longer valid. Most of the post-2022 stress gains are realized (assets bought cheap during the downturn). Leverage is also far lower than seen at peak levels. Since the ~33% returns in the past 3 years translate to just about double-digit total returns, the forward expectations should be in the range of 8-9% annually, with ~15% from income doing the heavy lifting and NAV drifting lower due to no material funding cost/spread improvement, while expenses remain high.

The income appears well covered, though (looking at NAV stability in the past few quarters), and the total return moderation is also not substantial - a couple of percentage points lower than the recent past, after the 2023-25 recovery.

A Decent Income Portfolio Addition

I downloaded the holdings from PDI's website and saw around a third of the portfolio in non-agency MBS and roughly another third in high-yield credit. Emerging market exposure shows up at around 10-15%. The current portfolio implications are that returns are mortgage and credit spread driven (as usual) and less impacted by long-duration rates.

The current spread environment appears benign. Additionally, rate volatility is contained, and funding conditions are stable. The best time to enter PDI (like almost everything else) is during peak stress, rather than normalization phases like we are in now. However, high single-digit total returns and ~15% yields should still be expected going ahead for fresh capital deployed today.
